data.title: Tencent: A Potential New Bull Market Is Taking Shape
data.description:
My last article about Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY) was published at the end of September 2024 at a time when we saw the first signs of optimism returning to the Chinese stock market. Back then, many major Chinese technology stocks rallied for a few short weeks. In my last article, I wrote in my conclusion:

And Tencent is certainly not the cheapest stock we can find in China – even among the major technology companies, other stocks seem cheaper. But Tencent is a high-quality business with a wide economic moat around and the possibility to grow with a high pace for a long time. Additionally, the stock is at least reasonably valued and even after the stock already increased 130% from its cyclical low, Tencent is still among the better investments one can make right now.

In my last article, I was a bit afraid that I was recommending buying a stock at a point when bullish sentiment was at its peak. And Tencent mostly declined in the months following the article. But since the second week of January, the stock started to rally again and is now trading about 10% higher compared to my last article.

The following article will take another look at Tencent and answer the question of whether the stock is still a “Buy”. But we start by looking at the macro picture in China – a topic that is coming up short in most articles.

The Bigger Picture

Before we talk about Tencent, we must look at the bigger picture. When talking about China and Chinese companies, we should not forget one very important aspect. From our Western point of view and focus on the United States, we always assume that the last few years were an economic successful time, with stock markets roaring and the economy growing with a solid pace.

However, the picture in China is different. Although China is still growing its GDP at a solid pace, it is not only lagging other Asian countries at this point, but also growing only in the low-to-mid single digits (a condition China is not really used to). And not only the economy is struggling, but the stock market was struggling as well. While the S&P 500 was running from all-time-high to all-time-high in the last few years, the Chinese CSI 300 index declined from almost 6,000 points to slightly above 3,000 points before it found its temporary bottom. This is coming close to a 50% crash, and we are talking about the same dimensions as the Great Financial Crisis in the United States.

These are numbers and statistics we must keep in mind when talking about China. But while I am rather bearish about the United States in the next few years, there is reason to be optimistic about China in the next few years. When looking at the chart above, there are first small signs that the CSI 300 found its double-bottom in 2024 and might be on its way to higher stock price again. And if the index should be able to climb above 6,000 points, it will complete a correction that lasted for almost 20 years. In 2007, the index already came close to 6,000 points for the first time and since 2007 we can see the Chinese stock market in a major correction. Of course, this is speculation, but we could be at the beginning of a long-lasting bull market in China.

Not only the stock market is sending signs of improvement, but the IMF also raised its GDP growth expectations for China again. In 2025, GDP growth is now expected to be 4.6% and in 2026 growth is expected to be 4.5%. We should not make the mistake to compare China in 2025 to the United States in 2025, but rather to the United States in 2009.

As I said, very speculative, and many unknowns remain (for example the negative impact a tariff war might have on China), but I would be rather bullish about the Chinese stock market at this point.

Quarterly Results

Tencent already reported third quarter results already in November 2024 and although the numbers can’t match growth rates Tencent reported five to ten years ago, we still see solid growth. Revenue increased from RMB 154,625 million in Q3/23 to RMB 167,193 million in Q3/24 – resulting in 8.1% year-over-year growth. Operating profit also increased from RMB 44,348 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 53,333 million in this quarter – resulting in 20.3% year-over-year growth and diluted earnings per share increased even 50.4% year-over-year from RMB 3.752 in Q3/23 to RMB 5.644 in Q3/24.

And finally, free cash flow – one of the most important metrics for any business – increased 14.5% year-over-year from RMB 51.1 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 58.5 billion this quarter. The combined MAUs of Weixin and WeChat also increased 3.4% YoY to $1,382 million on September 30, 2024. And fee-based VAS registered subscriptions increased even 9.1% YoY to 265 million.

When looking at the different segments, we still see one major segment continuing to struggle – FinTech and Business Services. And interestingly, this is the segment which was driving top line growth in the last few years. But in the last few quarters, revenue was rather stagnating and in Q3/24 revenue increased only 2.0% to RMB 53,089 million. Business Services grew mostly due to cloud services, and the increased efficiency of the cloud business also contributed to a 7-percentage point higher gross margin.

And when looking at the FinTech business, payment revenue decreased year-over-year, but wealth management service revenue increased as well as the number of users and aggregated customer assets. Over time, Tencent’s strategy is to convert more and more people into buying not just money market funds (the situation right now) but also longer-term equity-based lines and ETFs. Right now, Tencent is building these products and trying to provide the conversion mechanism for the people having equity.

VAS (Valued Added Services), on the other hand, grew revenue 9.2% year-over-year to RMB 82,695 million. This segment includes social networks, domestic games as well as international games. Revenue from social networks increased 4% year-over-year, mostly driven by a 20% increase in music subscription revenue, with the number of subscriptions increasing 16% year-over-year to 119 million. Domestic games also grew revenue 14% year-over-year, and international games grew 9% year-over-year (11% in constant currency). And while fintech is struggling right now, it was especially games struggling a few years back.

The third segment is Marketing Services (the segment was previously called Online Advertising), which reported the highest growth rates among the three segments. Revenue increased 16.6% year-over-year to RMB 29,993 million. Especially games and eCommerce advertising were driving growth, while real estate as well as food & beverage were rather struggling. The Olympic Games in Paris in 2024 also had a positive effect on advertising revenue.

Growth

In the last few quarters, top line growth improved again after it was close to zero or even negative in all four quarters of 2022. In the last few quarters, Tencent reported revenue growth rates in the mid-to-high single digits.

And for the next few years, analysts continue to be optimistic and assume growth rates even to accelerate a little bit. For the next four years until fiscal 2028, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 14.4%. When looking at the earnings revision data, we see analysts getting more optimistic about earnings per share over the last few quarters. I usually see this as a good sign, as it often means that analysts were too bearish and have to catch up to the “fundamental reality”. Additionally, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 14.4% in the next few years until fiscal 2028.

One of the top line growth drivers in the years to come will probably be the FinTech business segment again. Not only is Tencent trying to expand into new markets – like wealth management services – but we can also assume that the struggling payments business is recovering again. And different studies expect the market in China to grow at a high pace in the coming years, and Tencent should be able to grow at least in line with the overall market. Grand View Research is expecting the Chinese digital payment market to grow with a CAGR above 20%. And while this study might not be accurate anymore (date from 2022), Mordor Intelligence is expecting the market to grow with a CAGR of 8.4% until fiscal 2030. Statista is expecting a similar growth rate and is expecting the market to grow with a CAGR of 8.2% until 2029. Whatever the accurate number might be, we can assume at least high single digit growth rates in my opinion.

A second long-term driver of growth could be artificial intelligence. I already mentioned in my last article that Tencent is seeing artificial intelligence as a growth multiplier.

Recently, Tencent announced it will integrate DeepSeek’s AI model into WeChat (or Weixin in China) as a beta test, which led the shares to rise. In my last article I wrote that Tencent is, in my opinion, not among the major names regarding AI so far, but the company is among the leading technology companies in China and even though Tencent seems to be lagging a little bit, it can catch up in the years to come.

Although Tencent might not be at the forefront of developing AI models, it still can use the models of other companies – for example the model provided by DeepSeek – and integrate it to enhance productivity growth. It can integrate AI in its SaaS products and AI will also have a significant boost to its advertising business. Meta Platforms and other companies relying heavily on advertising and also reporting that AI is making advertising more efficient and more profitable.

Aside from growing the top line, Tencent is also improving its margins for its business segments over time, and we can assume that Tencent will be able to improve margins further. In the recent past, Tencent has improved its gross margin in an impressive way – from 44.1% in Q3/21 to 53.1% in Q3/24. While we should not assume similar improvements in the years to come, we can assume margin improvements to contribute in the low single digits to bottom line growth.

Finally, Tencent can also grow its bottom line by using share buybacks. And for a long time, Tencent did not buy back shares and the number of shares outstanding constantly increased. But in 2021, Tencent started to reduce the shares outstanding and although the share buybacks are not as aggressive as with some other companies, we can assume share buybacks to contribute about 1% or 2% growth to the bottom line in the years to come. As mentioned several times, Tencent is generating huge amounts of free cash flow and on September 30, 2024, Tencent had RMB 145.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents as well as RMB 198.0 billion in term deposits on its balance sheet (about $47 billion). Considering a market capitalization around $580 billion right now, this would be enough to repurchase about 8% of shares outstanding.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In the end, we must calculate an intrinsic value for Tencent to determine if the stock is still a good investment. When a stock increased a lot in value in a very short timeframe, one tends to ask if the stock is overvalued now. Since the low in 2023, Tencent’s stock price increased 147% and of course trading for a 2.5 times higher stock price can make a huge difference if a stock is fairly valued or not. On the other hand, we should not ignore that Tencent declined 75% from its previous all-time high and is still trading 36% below the 2021 highs. In the end, we should not look at previous highs or lows but calculate an intrinsic value by looking at fundamentals.

As always, we are using a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for Tencent. We are calculating with a 10% discount rate as this is the minimum annual return we like to achieve on all our investments. Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of shares outstanding, which was 9,238 million and take the free cash flow of the last four quarters as basis (RMB 184.9 billion).

Now we must make some estimates about realistic growth rates in the years to come. In my opinion, it is reasonable to calculate with 10% growth for the next ten years. And let’s be cautious and assume 4% terminal growth rates in 10 years from now for Tencent. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of HKD 531 and with a current stock price around HKD 490, Tencent is still slightly undervalued.

However, we can question how realistic these growth rates are and if there isn’t any reason to be even more optimistic. I already mentioned above that analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR around 14% in the next few years and when looking at the last five years, Tencent also grew revenue with a CAGR of 14% and operating income with a CAGR of almost 16%. Additionally, we should not forget that the last few years were rather challenging for the Chinese economy and for Tencent – making it not unlikely that Tencent is even able to grow with a higher pace again. Therefore, let’s be a little more optimistic and assume 12% growth for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of HKD 608 making Tencent already about 20% undervalued.

Finally, we can also ask how realistic 4% growth till perpetuity actually is for Tencent. I know that the CFI is suggesting not to calculate with higher terminal growth rates, but in the case of Tencent, we can make two strong arguments for at least 5% or even 6% growth till perpetuity. On the one hand, Tencent is an extremely diversified business with several structural advantages and therefore a very strong economic moat around its business. Second, the terminal growth rate should reflect GDP growth, as over time most business will only be able to grow in line with the overall economy. In the case of Tencent, however, we can make the argument that the Chinese economy will most likely be able to grow at a higher pace than the U.S. economy and 5% growth for the next few years seems not unreasonable.

When calculating an intrinsic value by assuming 12% growth for the next ten years followed by 5% growth till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of HKD 691 for Tencent. And when assuming even 6% terminal growth, the intrinsic value would be HKD 817. And in these two scenarios, Tencent would not only be undervalued, but could be classified as “bargain” once again.

Conclusion

I already wrote in my last article about Tencent, that it is not the cheapest stock among the big Chinese technology companies – including retail giants like JD.com (JD) and Alibaba (BABA) – but it is a Chinese powerhouse with an extremely strong moat. And I would still argue that Tencent is at least fairly valued and when using a little more optimistic assumptions, the stock is undervalued at this point. It depends on how optimistic we are that Tencent will continue to grow with a high pace. But Tencent remains a “Buy” in my opinion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WELL Health Technologies: Spin-Offs And Strategic Clarity Make This A Buy Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

WELL Health Technologies (TSX:WELL:CA), headquartered in Vancouver, is a leading Canadian digital health company that is focused on modernizing and improving healthcare through various technology means. WELL:CA operates a portfolio of primary care clinics, telehealth platforms, electronic medical record solutions, and cybersecurity tools for the healthcare sector. The company has been extremely active in relation to mergers & acquisitions, closing dozens of deals in 2024, and has several LOIs in the pipeline, along with spin-off plans.

WELL:CA recently had a run-up after strong Q3 earnings and provided more clarity on its spinoff plans for WELLSTAR. That subsidiary will be spun-off independently, and they raised $50MM from institutional investors to scale. Ultimately, I think that this stock has been stuck in the mud for over a year due to a lack of understanding from investors. While these moves have been a long time coming, WELL:CA is set up for a strong 2025 as their revenues and earnings will continue to soar and clarity on the company's strategy will come into focus. Without any pure play peers, I rate the stock as a Buy with a price target of $10 using both a DCF valuation and a sum of the parts multiple.

What To Look For During Earnings

WELL:CA will report Q4 results in late March, and all eyes will be on the company's plan to reshape its operations. I recommend investors look for a couple key things in the upcoming earnings. The first is its progress on its spin-off strategy. While WELLSTAR was announced as being spun-off in December, WELL:CA will still have a significant stake in the company. I'm curious to hear how management expects these moves to create value and how WELL:CA will continue to exert influence over the independent company as the majority owning shareholder. Additionally, management has been soured on its stock price and believes that additional value can be created through spin-offs of CRH Medical and better integration of Healwell AI (AIDX:CA), which they expect to buy out fully in 2025. More clarity must be needed to understand the vision of the company, especially since WELL:CA recently bought CRH Medical in 2021 as part of a US push. There are lots of moving parts and I recommend keeping a close eye reading between the lines when management take questions on the upcoming earnings call.

Another key thing that I'll be looking for are market share gains. The company is steadfast that the Canadian physician market is more fragmented and easily penetrable than the U.S. market, given its expertise across all provincial regulatory systems and the recent start of privatization by the conservative Ontario government. Therefore, in alignment with understanding their strategy and spin-off plans, understanding better how they will be able to penetrate the Canadian physician market and create meaningful synergies will be important to know. In December 2024, WELL acquired Jack Nathan Health's Canadian clinical assets, adding 72 clinics and expanding its reach to within Walmart Canada locations. That said, the company does not have clinics in all provinces, and to attain scale, must better integrate its technology solutions with care-providers to obtain significant revenue growth. More clarity on those plans and progress will be critical for investors to better understand, and subsequently invest, in WELL:CA.

Canadian Industry

I expect WELL:CA to pivot its focus on conquering Canada before utilizing capital on other large U.S. acquisitions, based on management's recent commentary. The primary care clinic market is a significant part of Canada's healthcare system, with physician spending accounting for ~$48Bn annually. But despite its size, the market remains highly fragmented, presenting significant opportunities for consolidation and modernization. WELL:CA owns about 1% of this market share, operating over 185 clinics across Canada. With revenue of under a billion, this leaves the company with a ton of runway to consolidate. While TELUS (TU) has a robust technology platform, there are no operators with over 20% market share in this space. It's been difficult for large operators to gain scale for numerous reasons. This strategy is capital intensive and involves acquiring existing clinics (i.e. assuming the leases, staff, and operations), while centralizing administrative functions to drive efficiencies. WELL:CA has the platform to do this, but it takes time to achieve meaningful scale and EBITDA efficiency in these clinics through onboarding so many small clinics into a centralized platform. However, by addressing common pain points, such as regulatory compliance, technology integration, and back-office management, healthcare providers can better focus on patient care while the parent organization optimizes operational performance, and WELL:CA is banking on being the first to do it to see strong results.

Risks

The company has to contend with several key risks that investors should be aware of. Operating within Canada's very regulated healthcare space, WELL:CA is subject to abrupt changes in provincial healthcare funding and data privacy laws, which could affect margins (reimbursement rates) and operational effectiveness. Ontario is going to the polls on Feb. 28, and if the Liberals or NDP win, I would expect new barriers built that would stymie WELL:CA's consolidation strategy. The company's success also hinges on retaining experienced physicians and medical staff; while the company believes that their offerings are significantly better, the rapid change may lead to information losses and lack of trust. Finally, the M&A heavy strategy may lead to additional unforeseen integration costs; investors should know that this company is looking to take over the health market in Canada and may not succeed inorganically.

Model Shows Upside

WELL:CA stock roared to close 2024, but has been treading water in early 2025, and I'm really interested in management's guidance for this year. In terms of expected growth, the company has the cash it needs for CAPEX and other uses, but depending on their M&A strategy, they may require more funding to scale effectively. I forecast a cost of capital of 9.6%, with the current capital structure expected to remain similar in the near term, a cost of debt around 6.5% given their current rates, a beta of 1.19, a market risk premium of 5.5%, and a size premium of 1% given their status as a small/mid-cap stock.

I forecast an enterprise value of ~$2.7Bn, with a revenue increase of 13% in 2025 and a going forward revenue CAGR of 7.4% over a 5-year term. In my base case, I hold other costs as more or less in line with recent results, as the company has remained consistent from an operational perspective - while new acquisitions are less accretive, old ones become more accretive exponentially. A $10 share price (see below) can be supported with a 12x exit multiple. My upside-downside based on DCF is $5-$13, which is a range based on various assumptions to revenues, margins, exit multiples, and capital structure.

From a sum of the parts multiple, I expect around $9 a share, but I don't include the company's AIDX stake given the uncertainty if WELL:CA will buy them out. I weight my DCF more significantly than the sum of the parts, as I expect clarity in vision will allow the firm to be viewed from a fundamentals perspective shortly.

Bottom Line

WELL:CA stock had a strong end to 2024, core operating segments remain solid. However, investors are unclear of their strategy and I expect upcoming earnings in March to provide better color. While risks are there, I think the stock is worth more than the sum of the parts, and I expect the stock to approach $10 over the next year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Genco Shipping: Take Advantage Of The Seasonal Low
data.description:
Genco (NYSE:GNK), a global shipper of drybulk cargo, recently came out with Q4 2024 results, which also announced a reduction in the quarterly dividend from $0.40 to $0.30. Nevertheless, I think today's price is a good one, at a discount to book value and current dividend yield over 8%. Moreover, investors will be getting in at a seasonal low point in the Baltic Drybulk Index (BDIY:IND), and therefore sufficient room for upside on the cash flows and dividend exists.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote last June, shares traded at $21, but I still rated it a Buy as a long-term investment. I cited the following advantages:

  • Near-elimination of outstanding debt
  • Vastly improved cost structure
  • Minimum yield of 3.8% at the time (5.5% currently)
  • Healthy balance between supramax and capesize vessel cycles

The slide above essentially shows how they balance their capital allocation, in a value-driven, accretive strategy. This was the basis for my Buy rating.

Recent Decline in the BDI

The Baltic Drybulk Index ("BDI") is one of the main drivers of Genco's operating results, as it reflects spot prices on shipping rates. This influences not only the revenues they collect with the existing fleet but how they prioritize the capital allocation I described above.

If we look above, we see that the BDI was much higher at the end of summer, more than twice what it is currently. As spot prices were much lower through Q4 and continued to decline into 2025, it's not a surprise that the dividend was reduced from $0.40 to $0.30.

If we compare the current BDI value of 941 to the past decade, very few excerpts of the past are lower. The recent low on January 30th was 715. The lows in 2016 were about 300, by comparison.

Adjusted for inflation, this would be like a BDI level of 402 today. Considering how the BDI can be as high as 2000, spot prices could be lower, however likely that may be. In the Q4 earnings call, management mainly attributed the lows to seasonality, which isn't uncommon around this point in the year.

If you look at the consolidated image above, it's very common for the BDI to reach a low point by late January or into February and then ride a large increase into the spring and summer. Management therefore expects spot prices will pick up, and I agree.

Genco's Response

While full-year results were an improvement over 2023, the lower BDI levels in Q4 had an observable, year-over-year impact.

This is why Q4 2024 reported lower revenues than Q4 2023, while the rest of 2024 carried the year. Both of these Q4s show how Genco takes advantage.

In 2023, Genco halted its de-leveraging and actually took on some debt to acquire two capesize vessels. They did this same move in 2024. After paying down debt in the first three quarters, they took on bit more to purchase another cape vessel.

The BDI being a composite index of all drybulk, Genco's reasoning is easier to see if capesize and supramax are broken up (which you can see in the slide above). There is more volatility on the part cape, and during the earnings call management explained that the lulls allow them to make much cheaper acquisitions of cape vessels.

The lower BDI levels into 2025 likely explain the dividend cut that just happened, as 2024's distributable cash flow of $12.9M was consistent (on a quarterly basis) with the $50.4M distributed in the first three quarters.

In the earnings call, management referred to the voluntary reserve they keep to smooth out the dividend during lulls, noting that enough cash exists to distribute $0.45 per share. They also brought up the lulls in 2023, with the quarterly distribution going as low as $0.15.

In my view, this reflects what Genco is minimally capable of doing (something I asserted in my last thesis). I even think $0.20 per share is still possible, especially if they want to make use of that reserve.

As we are more than halfway through Q1 with a much lower BDI, it remains possible that next distribution will still be lower than the $0.40 levels being distributed last year, but I believe those levels will return once seasonal trends improve.

Risks

One of the risks that could impact Genco's long-term results are the recent imposition of tariffs by the Trump Administration.

Grain is supramax cargo, and this would be the biggest potential impact to Genco.

At the moment, China has not retaliated with its own grain tariffs, so even that danger is speculative. Trump did impose a flat 25% tariff on all steel imports. This could be disruptive for Genco and other shippers. As the supramax vessels have a lot more alternatives (in cargo and routes) that they can ship, I expect a minimal impact to Genco's top line. The main concern is if this continues to escalate and involve more countries.

Capesize cargo (iron and coal) could still be a big impact, but exports from the US to China generally haven't been Genco's focus. Their cape typically involved exports from Brazil, West Africa, and Australia into China/East Asia. Unless these areas get sucked into America's trade war, I also expect any negative impact from tariffs to be minimal.

My bigger concern isn't therefore the direct and immediate impact to Genco's shipping. Rather, it's that increased costs in the global economy from tariffs could slow consumption and indirectly hurt shipping that way.

The domino effect may not be immediate, but if the BDI is pushed down further by reduced global demand, Genco will be treading near its breakeven rate. If this were to occur, I don't expect the company would fail with their balance sheet, but the dividend may be suspended entirely until the BDI increases again.

Valuation

Currently, I think GNK is attractively valued for over an 8% yield, even after the recent cut. As book value per share is $21.49, I also believe it trades at a very safe discount.

With only about $80M in debt against $1B in assets, this isn't a distressed balance sheet, and I believe the discount provides good value. It's not a valuation, in my view, that requires us to overthink too much. What you see is what you get.

Conclusion

Genco continues to be an example of how good capital management creates opportunities to move throughout shipping cycles. This was most recently reflected in their cape vessel acquisitions. Despite a dividend cut amid lower spot prices, the current yield is still an attractive 8%, and shares trade at about two-thirds book value per share.

While trade wars could reduce global demand for shipping, even in directly, Genco has at least taken the proper steps to prepare its balance sheet for such challenges. Because the value is too easy and the risk profile low, I maintain my Buy rating today.

data.title: I Love Palantir But I'm Not Stubborn: Dropping My Rating, For Now, To 'Hold'
data.description:
Sometimes you have to eat your words.

A comment in response to my 2/11/25 Amazon.com (AMZN) writeup posed an interesting question:

Based on this article, you seem to be very passionate convicted about AMZN! What are your top 5 stocks that you are currently most passionate about and could be considered as your 5 convicted list? Thanks.

My answer on 2/13 included Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) as one of my choices. I referred to it as “one of the most effective users of AI.”

Mm-mm good. Yum. Delicious. Beautiful words, and great tasting too.

Among the worst kept secrets in the investment community generally and the Seeking Alpha community in particular are…

  • PLTR’s stock valuation metrics are breathtakingly high. We might have to hitch a ride on SpaceX to get a close-up view. This issue has been discussed for a lot and more so since PLTR beat December quarter estimates, causing another rocket booster to fire.
  • New Defense Secretary Peter Hegseth, consistent with his being part of the new Trump-DOGE culture, reportedly asked those under him to propose 8% budget cuts over the next five years. That broke the rally in PLTR shares.

As to my gourmet words, I laid out that meal’s ingredients in my 11/10/24 PLTR writeup.

I headlined it: “I'm Into Value, I Avoided The DotCom Bubble, But I Still Say 'Buy' For Palantir.”

Numerically speaking, that recommendation still looks great. The stock is up 68%. The S&P 500 is about even.

If the stock market were to permanently end today, I’d declare victory and go home. But that’s not how this works. With homage to Shakespeare and Macbeth, there’s always “tomorrow, tomorrow and tomorrow.”

So I need to reiterate or modify my rating.

I’m going to change it to “Hold.”

This doesn’t mean my conviction about Palantir, the company, has diminished. It remains high as ever, possibly even more so.

But companies and their stocks aren’t one and the same. We need separate opinions for each.

I’m not joining those who say “Sell” or “Strong Sell” for PLTR. There’s much more nuance here than have been acknowledged in any of the bearish reports I’ve seen.

But based on an investment framework I like to use, the Keynesian Beauty Contest (see below), I can’t really stay bullish at this time.

So here now are the cons and pros to PLTR… and my neutral resolution.

The Cons – The Valuations

It’s one thing to say the ratios are high. But some things simply have to be seen to be fully appreciated.

(NOTE: I compare PLTR to medians among companies in the Application Software industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

But here’s the nuance I’m not seeing among the bears…

Those ratios are meaningless if the fundamentals on which they’re based (EPS, Sales, whatever) aren’t accurate.

If we could rely on historical numbers… no problem. But that’s very far from ideal. We really need estimated future numbers.

This isn’t mere bullish hype. It lies at the heart of stock valuation. It’s all based on the present value of expected future cash flows.

That’s complicated. The future doesn’t mean one year, two years, etc. It means however long we intend to hold the stock.

Unlike with bonds, there’s no clear answer for stocks. Academic models, like dividend growth or discounted cash flow (DCF), use infinite holding periods. (Even one who does a 10-year cash flow forecast still needs to add on a terminal value calculation… based on infinity.)

So when I see folks argue, “PLTR’s valuation can’t work unless it can grow 25% a year for, say, 10 or 20 years” (sorry I can’t provide specific links; I can’t recall specific articles right now), my reaction is, "Yeah, so … that’s the very least bona fide valuation models are supposed to consider".

Moreover, what makes anybody assume a company like PLTR can’t grow that quickly, or perhaps even more vigorously… and for a very long time!

PLTR is an emerging company. It’s been around since 2003. That alone is pretty new. But the most exciting part of the company are newer even than that. (I’m referring to its AIP business, which started in 2023.)

Emerging companies don’t follow “normal” revenue and earnings patterns. They lose money early on. They grow at accelerated rates. (It’s like humans... We grow, in percentage terms, much faster when younger than age 5 than we do when we’re in our 50s.)

This is especially so on the bottom line when the operating profits rise past fixed costs.

That’s what’s happening to PLTR now. (See the “Risks” section below.)

Here’s a table I published in connection with my 1/12/25 Rigetti (RGTI) writeup.

It’s a sampling of early super-normal growth rates logged by some of today’s familiar giants.

And as I’ll discuss below, PLTR’s business seems even more powerful. I think it’s reasonable to expect even more growth from it.

In sum, the fundamentals on which PLTR’s valuations are based are very tenuous. So, too, are the ratios.

I discussed more about this in my initial PLTR writeup and in my 7/13/24 AMZN writeup (wherein I explained why noted value guru William Miller was an early investor there). I also discussed why the present is nothing like the circa-2000 dot-com bubble. Please refer there for more.

For now, know that the valuation-based bear case against PLTR is far softer than it seems at first glance.

That said, it still qualifies as a negative for PLTR stock because many on the Street take the ratios seriously. (See below.)

I’ve already hinted at the pros (super-normal growth). So let’s now jump fully into that topic.

The Pros – The Business

We’re considering PLTR. We’re not looking at Walmart (WMT).

If you want WMT, or any other company whose fundamentals can be statistically analyzed in light of standard economic metrics, then look at WMT or whatever.

PLTR is a newly emerging business... advanced analytics. More than that it’s new, or rather newer, wrinkle on that... it’s now about AI too.

And it’s not just any AI. PLTR doesn’t merely manage your personal calendar, or summarize your e-mails.

I have a free version of Microsoft’s (MSFT) Pilot on my iPad… and I still can’t figure out what I want to ask it to do.

Alphabet’s (GOOGL) search-page AI summaries are nice. But they don’t enhance my productivity… I still check the primary sources.

Ditto the latter’s presumably AI-based separation of my Gmail Inbox into Primary, Promotional, Social and Updates. Because I see it’s often inaccurate in classifying emails as I’d like, I still have to look at everything before I can delete.

And don’t get me started on AI customer service bots. I have yet to complete even one transaction using any of these. I always wind up needing a human representative.

I’m not bothered by any of this. I understand AI is still in its primitive stage. I know it will get much better over time.

Seeking Alpha’s Joe Albano said it best when he wrote about the AI revenue funnel. I described it in my last PLTR article. By way of quick summary…

First come components, such as those for which Nvidia (NVDA) got rich and famous. Next, come companies that put the component together, as in Dell’s (DELL) AI servers. Last come the end uses… as discussed above with Pilot, Google stuff, and bots.

That’s why I’m OK with efforts of firms like MSFT and GOOGL being as primitive as they are. They’re at the end of the funnel. They’re time to really shine will come. But that day isn’t yet here.

PLTR is a grand exception.

Like MSFT and GOOGL, it comes last in the revenue funnel. But unlike with MSFT and GOOGL, PLTR’s end-user AI applications are seriously valuable and productive to its clients. It takes on big, important tasks, and executes them quickly and effectively.

Management discussed some examples on the company’s 2/3/25 earnings call:

  • PLTR helped a major American pharmacy chain automatically load-balance prescription fulfillment and orchestrate patient outreach.
  • It helped an American telecom company manage and accelerate its decommissioning of old network technologies and equipment while achieving big cost savings relating to this process.
  • A leading global insurance company that had already used PLTR to automate underwriting workflows expanded its deal and would up cutting a two-week process to three hours.
  • PLTR helped Panasonic Energy North America expand an AI program it created for the latter by now supporting 350 technicians producing 5.5 million batteries per day, cutting machine downtime, increasing throughput, and more rapidly onboarding new technicians.
  • Using PLTR’s Warp Speed initiative, defense technology company Anduril achieved up to 200-fold improvement in anticipating and addressing supply shortages.
  • Mining company Rio Tinto expanded an existing relationship with PLTR to now get automated access to unstructured data and quickly address problems the client once deemed too complex.
  • Network Rail used PLTR to coordinate 53 driverless trains, each with 240 wagons while improving overall throughput and safety.

Here’s what’s really most impressive about these examples.

First, they address tasks that are fairly boring aspects of the clients’ operations, yet they are all cost saving and/or time saving. What company doesn’t want that!

From these examples, I infer that practically any business anywhere (except maybe oddballs that don’t want to optimize profitability) could benefit from what PLTR offers.

Second is the fact that its management goes over such case studies on earnings calls.

Do Apple (AAPL) earnings calls review individual iPhone customers who benefit from that product’s capabilities? Does Walmart management call out customers who got what they wanted at a good price at one of its stores?

Those are, by now, ordinary mundane “dog-bites-man” non-news stories.

That PLTR even considers doing this, and that analysts don’t laugh, is testament to how new and out of the norm this still is. These are still-rare “man-bites-dog” newsworthy stories.

So … imagine how many more potential customers and comparable experiences lie ahead in PLTR’s future.

It boggles the mind!

Competition will, of course, increase… so I assume.

But it won’t be easy to square off against PLTR. Imagine companies that create today’s imbecilic customer service bots trying to go after customers also considering PLTR.

Consider the latter’s now-multi-decade heritage of advanced (pre-AI) analytics, that date back to before analytics became hip. Consider, too, PLTR’s heritage in serving U.S. Defense clients, those with the most urgent and sensitive needs.

Undoubtedly, some will succeed in taking business away from PLTR. Nobody has or keeps a 100% market share.

But seriously, I cannot see how anyone can credibly suggest for PLTR any sort of “normal” growth rate projection within any reasonable time horizon.

That doesn’t mean every single quarter will outpace the last. Clients adopt things at their own paces. So the timing of future work can get lumpy. But in terms of the big picture… So what!

And, by the way, speaking of defense, let’s think about the Hegseth cost-cutting memo.

Seeking Alpha News said on 2/16/25:

Included on the chopping block will be excessive bureaucracy and spending on programs related to climate change and diversity, equity and inclusion, Bloomberg News reported, citing unnamed sources. Such proposals would be in line with President Donald Trump’s efforts.

We can’t know for sure until details come out. And we do have to acknowledge that the Trump Administration so far has been a high-speed whirlwind of Executive Orders, announcements, and news stories.

So for now, all things remain possible. But frankly, the above quote does not seem to strike at the heart of PLTR’s bailiwick.

And generally, my bullishness on PLTR stems mainly from what I expect it to do in the future among commercial customers.

So to me, a major positive for PLTR was and still is, the company’s business story.

Let’s now consider…

PLTR’s Business Numbers

I’ll say up front that what you’ll see here is good… really, really, really good.

Lets’ start with some highlights.

Here’s some customer count details to help put the latter table in perspective.

Let’s move on to segment financials.

Government remains bigger… owing to PLTR’s defense-work heritage. But Commercial is already up to a very meaningful level. And the latter is growing more quickly.

Here, now are the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

Aside from the reality that PLTR stock is hanging around the $100 mark (raising the much discussed valuation issues), those quarterlies are quite fine.

Here, now, are summaries of historical and forward growth rates.

About those NM (“Not Meaningful”) items you see for 3Y historical figures… PLTR has been transitioning from losses to profits in terms of GAAP (standard accounting figures that, unlike what I showed in the quarterly EPS table, do not eliminate non-operating items). When that happens, growth rate computations tend to look whacky.

Here’s something that also looks whacky but this time in a good sense of the word, PLTR’s core fundamentals.

Notice how debt-light PLTR is!

Its return on equity (ROE), while beating the daylights out of industry peers, is below that of the SPY stock universe.

Bear in mind lots of debt helps boost ROE. (When companies use a lot of borrowed capital to generate earnings, those earnings look much higher in percent terms when measured on the smaller equity base.) So debt-light PLTR’s ROE should be lower than that of the more debt-heavy SPY group.

With return on assets, which is neutral regarding debt, PLTR is beating both benchmarks.

Bottom line: The numbers match the qualitative story. Both are terrific.

Risk

I usually show the table below for small companies and/or those where financial strength is questionable. Neither applies to PLTR.

But I’ll show it anyway as incidental to valuation risk.

Many today amplify that risk by citing the turn-of-the-century dot-com bubble and crash. In my prior PLTR report, I explained the huge differences between then and now.

Here’s where PLTR stands.

What’s not to love here?

PLTR is metaphorically printing cash, not burning it.

And fixed-cost coverage is growing briskly. R&D is rising. But Revenues are growing much faster. So the R&D-to-Revenues ratio is falling.

And look at the operating margin trend! This sort of thing reflects a company poised to grow EPS at a rate that far exceeds the WMTs of the world.

(It seems, here, like I’m picking on WMT. I don’t mean to do that. But it’s hard to avoid this when so many vocal bears cite valuation ratios and analytic frameworks more appropriate to mature companies like that, than to something like PLTR.)

It warrants repeating here how different PLTR is from the cash-burning dumpster-fire companies that imploded back around 2000.

What to do About PLTR Stock

I usually lead off this section with a valuation table. I already showed that above. And I think we can all agree those numbers are insanely high.

The issue is whether PLTR deserves that kind of valuation.

Unfortunately, there is no clear-cut logical way to answer this. We can model and forecast to our hearts’ content.

But it’s all guesswork.

That’s the downside of building a new business in a new field. We lack precedents. We have no legitimate data.

I tried above to give you a sense of how untapped and potentially vast the market is for what PLTR sells. But I can’t put a number on it.

The best anybody can do is evaluate the story and the credibility of the business, as William Miller did with AMZN back in the 1990s.

But through it all, there is a number for PLTR’s stock price that is, and will later seem obvious in retrospect, way too high.

I can’t pinpoint that, either.

There’s only one thing I can think of to do…

I’ll apply the behavioral-finance-based Keynesian Beauty Contest approach.

My opinion counts here. But my view is only one of countless other opinions that, rightly or wrongly, impact supply and demand for PLTR shares and ultimately, its price.

My role is akin to that of a member of a panel that judges a beauty contest. But I don’t pick my favorite contestant. Instead, I choose the contestant I think will, rightly or wrongly, be chosen by most of the other judges.

I believe my view of PLTR’s business and fundamentals is pretty widely shared.

But when it comes to stock price and valuation, it looks to me like the majority of judges are leaning away from my view.

This backing away is apparent from today’s rhetoric. And it shows in the most recent price bars. It also shows in the tiny downturn in the heretofore bullishly trending 10-day exponential moving average (EMA).

I also look at Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators remain very solidly bullish. So based only on that, I’d say buyers being more motivated than sellers exerts upward pressure on stock prices.

But I’m not a doctrinaire chartist. I read these in context of everything going on in the rest of the relevant world.

On that basis, I think the better part of valor right now is to notice and respect the increasing frowns among so many other judges on the Keynesian panel. CMF and CO are multi-period indicators. I expect them to soften going forward, as the stock loses some steam.

I disagree with the other Keynesian judges. But I don’t have enough buying power to overpower the impact of their collective view.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

Accordingly, I now believe PLTR will be an average, rather than above-average performer.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by lowering PLTR from “Buy” to “Hold.”

(I think PLTR’s business strengths are sufficiently pronounced to attract more buyers if the stock falls too far. That’s why I’m not going down to a bearish rating.)

data.title: The BTC Mining Industry: Running To Stand Still
data.description:
“When a management with a reputation for brilliance tackles a business with a reputation for poor fundamental economics, it is the reputation of the business that remains intact.” –Warren Buffett

The BTC mining industry

In my opinion, BTC (BTC-USD) mining is a C+ business and investors should recognize the importance of industry dynamics when investing. BTC miners are often thought of as levered plays on BTC, where, like the tide, the price of BTC lifts or lowers all boats. However, most BTC miners have underperformed BTC in the last year despite several positive catalysts, most important being the increase in the price of BTC. Investors should be cautious and recognize the cyclical nature of the industry is just as important (if not more so) than the qualities of individual companies. And even companies with positive underlying fundamentals usually lose money due to the poor economics of the industry.

BTC mining is a straight-forward business where profit is determined by the difference between Bitcoin’s spot price and power and computing costs. For example, if the spot price of BTC is $100,000 and power are computing costs are $70,000, a BTC miner will make $30,000 per BTC.

It’s a simple business, but it is not easy. BTC mining is very competitive with low barriers to entry, and despite having low energy costs, miners typically have low margins. For example, none of them made any money last year! Gross margins were positive, but net incomes were negative after including depreciation expense for computers. Because P/E ratios are undefined, EBITDA and CFO multiples are two financial metrics most commonly used for comparison and valuation. EBITDA adds back depreciation expense, which some argue is overstated due to a longer useful life of the computers. However, not replacing miners will eventually result in higher energy costs, so either way, computer costs impact earnings. Cash flow from operations (CFO) were also negative– except for a handful of miners. Despite negative CFOs, many miners recycle cash flows into new machines and additional mining capacity to remain competitive. Thus, most miners are running to stand still financially, and investors should question the logic of additional investment.

In this article, I use three attributes to differentiate between BTC miners: 1) operational efficiency; 2) whether the miner keeps the BTC it mines or sells it (i.e. a HODL strategy); and 3) whether the miner plans to pivot towards HPC/AI datacenters. Each of these differentiators had positive catalysts last year that caused individual BTC miners to perform well in the short term. However, on the whole BTC miners have underperformed during a bull cycle for BTC. If miners cannot make money during the best of times, it’s certain that they will lose money in the worst of times.

A final criticism investors should be aware of is that most miners use stock offerings and convertible bonds to pursue growth at all cost, diluting existing shareholders. So, although the company might have an advantage in the differentiators above (i.e. better profitability, an increase in HODL value, and a pivot to HPC), the equity value of the company will decline because the company continually requires an infusion of new equity to maintain its market position.

In conclusion, the economics of BTC mining sort of stinks, and investors should be cautious of the cyclical nature of the industry before investing.

Current BTC Bull cycle and positive and negative catalysts:

Last year, BTC miners benefitted from multiple positive catalysts that ushered in a new bull cycle. Most important for the bull cycle was an increase in price of BTC. The price of BTC is a positive catalyst for BTC miners for two reasons. First is that it improves margins for the mining business, and second, the price of BTC increases the value of the HODL if the miner holds BTC. As shown in the chart below, the current BTC cycle appears to be progressing similar to previous cycles.

Source: Where are we in the bitcoin cycle?

A second positive catalyst for BTC and miners last year was Trump’s election in November. It is widely expected that Trump’s administration will be positive for the BTC industry with favorable regulations, favorable energy policy, and the potential for a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR). A third catalyst for BTC has been investor demand for BTC ETFs and corporate adoption of BTC as a treasury asset. Most prominent in this area is Michael Saylor and Strategy (MSTR) purchasing BTC using convertible debt. A final positive catalyst for BTC miners is the creation of a BTC miner ETF by Grayscale. The Grayscale Bitcoin Miners ETF (MNRS) will likely increase investment and interest in the industry. To promote their ETF, Grayscale wrote this summary of the industry.

Despite the positive catalysts, most BTC miners have underperformed BTC over the last year. For example, the chart below shows miners' performance through the year when compared to BTC.

Roughly half the BTC miners posted gains over the last year. However, only Core Scientific, Bitdeer, Hut8, and TeraWulf gained more than BTC, with Iris Energy, and Cipher posting returns similar to BTC.

Lastly, the chart below shows the hypothetical return of $100 invested in BTC when compared to an index of 12 BTC miners. Although BTC was up about 83% over the past year, the BTC miner index was only up about 50%.

It can be argued that the returns above are somewhat arbitrary due to the selection of the 1-year, historical time window (i.e. February 2024 - 2025). However, most miners lost money when looking at their financials over the past year.

In general, miners briefly made money in Q1 as the increase in BTC price improved margins. However, the BTC halving in the second quarter was a negative catalyst that decreased revenues and returned miners to negative net incomes. As a result, twelve trailing month earnings appear positive for some miners (MARA, RIOT, and HUT), but I doubt that miners will report positive earnings in 2025 unless BTC continues to rise, further increasing margins. The impact of the halving can also be seen in EBITDA and CFO in Q3, which are negative for all but three miners (CORZ, WULF and BITF).

A second negative catalyst has been an increase in global hash rates, which have increased 50% from about 550 TH/s to 850 TH/s over the last year. Thus, miners that have not increased their production in proportion to the increase in global has rate will mine less BTC per day. Consequently, miners only make money during these short time windows where the BTC price increases, before global hash rates respond to erode (if not eliminate) temporary profits. It will be interesting to watch revenues and income for Q4 2024 and the price of BTC going forward.

A framework for comparing miners

The three attributes I use to differentiate miners are: operational efficiency, the miners’ HODL strategy, and whether it plans to pivot to more stable revenues of HPC. Despite these attributes as a way to evaluate miners, the industry dynamics are likely still more important. As the Warren Buffett quote suggests, an A+ operator in a C+ industry will likely lose.

Operational Efficiency

For operational efficiency, power cost and computer efficiency are the two most important metrics. However, it is really difficult to rank miners by operational efficiency because there are a variety of metrics, which often indicate contradictory performance among miners. Also, I think there is little differentiation in operational performance because power costs and miner efficiency are somewhat uniform. For power costs, miners have established operations around the world near the cheapest sources of power. Often these are stranded, excess energy resources, such as hydropower, nuclear power, natural gas flared from a fracking, or intermittent wind and solar. A miner might get a good deal of 3-4 cents/kWh for electricity, but there is a natural, lower limit for power costs because power producers have to cover their own capital costs of energy production. Good miners have fixed price power agreements behind the meter, where a bad miner will have variable, ‘front of the meter’ power costs. Second, there is little differentiation for computer efficiency because miners all buy the same computers from the same computer three companies (Bitmain, Canaan, and MicroBT). Although machines might become older and less efficient, most miners upgrade their machines after 2–3 years, and sell the older machines. So a single miner never has a significant advantage for computer efficiency for a substantial period of time. In some ways, miners are like Formula 1 race car teams - they all have the same car design, engine, tires, etc. One team might have a slight advantage for a short period, but the other miners will recognize their advantage and upgrade their machines to eliminate the advantage.

Below are three metrics I follow to compare miner’s operational efficiency: BTC miner per exahash; energy efficiency (J/TH) of mining operations; and quarterly mining costs per BTC mined. The table is ranked by market cap, which tends to coincide with the exahash of mining capacity, and thus, the amount of BTC mined per month. Collectively this group of publically traded companies is about $30 billion of market cap, and represents about 31% of the BTC mined on any given day, or roughly 3,900 BTC in December.

BTC mined per EH/s and energy efficiency in J/TH are the two best metrics for efficiency. BTC mined per EH/s measures how productive the miner fleet is with each exahash of capacity. Larger numbers are better for this metric, and the best miners report about 18-19 BTC mined per EH. The second is energy efficiency, which measures the amount of energy per unit of computing power. Smaller numbers are better for this metric, with the most efficient miners under 20 J/TH. The final metric is quarterly mining costs for electricity per BTC mined. This metric shows the electrical cost of each BTC mined. Lower cost for each BTC means a miner will have higher gross margins, and likely higher net incomes. Similarly, the final column shows reported energy costs per MWh. The best miners tend to have contracted power between $30-40 MWh (or $0.03-0.04 per kWh). As part of their power agreements, most miners have curtailment obligations, which require miners to decrease their power consumption when demand and power rates increase. As a secondary data point to my numbers below, Anthony Power wrote a great article recently that reconciles capacity, efficiency and power costs across miners.

HODL

The second differentiator between BTC miners is whether it keeps the BTC it mines, dubbed a HODL strategy (or hang on for dear life). Due to the price volatility of BTC, some miners historically sold all the BTC it mined to fund operations and report more consistent earnings. Executives of the no HODL strategy are no doubt BTC advocates, but argue that the business of mining and speculation on BTC are two distinct concepts. As they say, ‘If you believe in BTC, buy BTC. But our business is mining, not speculating on the price of BTC.’

Other miners historically sold the bare minimum to fund operations and HODLed the BTC they mined. As a result, historical revenues were lower, but now many of these miners have substantial BTC reserves on the balance sheet. These reserves add leverage, where the market price of BTC adds to or subtracts from the value of the company. Given there will only be 21 million BTC, and that likely 20 million remain due to loss of keys, these HODLs that were established at much lower BTC prices and could potentially exceed the value of the mining operations. As shown in the far-right column, in some cases the mining operations only represent 50% of the value of the company, with the other 50% of the value due to the HODL.

To put the HODLs below into context, collectively the group owns about 90,000 BTC. BlackRock BTC ETF owns about 580,000 BTC and Strategy owns about 400,000 BTC, or about 3% and 2% respectively (or 5% in total) of all BTC that will exist. So, the miners are roughly one-quarter the size of the Strategy or BlackRock ETFs, with half of the industry’s HODL owned by Marathon. If the US or some other nation pursues a BTC Reserve and attempts to acquire 500,000 BTC (similar in size to IBIT or MSTR), it will be very difficult to source enough BTC for the reserve without the price of BTC going much higher. In short, miners cannot supply enough BTC from their future mining operations. As mentioned, the miners collectively mine only 3,800 BTC per month. So a BTC reserve of 400,000 BTC would take 100 months to mine, or 9 years of all miners’ production! In my opinion, many BTC miners with HODLs do not currently reflect this upside potential from the price of BTC. As a sub-set of the HODL strategy, some miners also issue convertible debt to purchase BTC in addition to the BTC they mine, implementing a strategy similar to Strategy.

HPC Pivot

The last differentiator between BTC miners is whether the miner is expanding into high-performance computing (HPC) to leverage their expertise building data centers and power agreements to increase revenues. The advantage of the HPC strategy is that HPC revenues will be more stable, have better margins, and allow for added leverage. Core Scientific set off this trend last summer when it announced a deal to host HPC operations for Core Weave, an AI/ML software developer. The deal has sort of set a high-water mark where Core Scientific will receive $1.5m for each MW of power with 80% margins on the capital invested. TeraWulf was the second company to announce a deal in December 2024. Other miners had hosting operations already (BTDR), or recently acquired data centers (BTBT) to enter the HPC space. Riot platforms initially stated they would not host the HPC market, but announced in January that they would pivot to HPC and decrease its energy target for its BTC mining operations. Some people speculate this indicates Riot is close to announcing a deal for its energy assets.

Miners that do not intend to pivot to HPC argue that miners lack the financial firepower to compete with tech companies and hyper scalers. For example, the combined market cap of all the miners is $30 billion. Microsoft alone plans to spend $80 billion in capital expenditures in 2025 for AI, or roughly 2.5 times the market cap of the entire miner industry. The Wall Street Journal wrote an article recently that summarized $75 billion in quarterly capex at the tech giants. Also, it is unclear if miners have a unique competitive advantage for hosting compared to other data center operators. Indeed, they have experience building BTC mining facilities, but ultimately most miners plan to offer building shells with power and an internet connection with the customer providing the computers. How exactly is this different than a data center REIT, like Digital Realty Trust (DLR)? (More specified build outs and hosting arrangements are more complex, require server GPU computers, and carry more risk due to computer obsolescence.)

Conclusion

I hope with this article provided some insight into the industry and provides a warning about some mistakes I’ve made.

I first invested in HIVE Digital (HIVE) during the BTC bull cycle that began in 2016-2017. It is a small position that I continue to hold. Over this time frame, BTC has increased 50x, rising from $2,000 to $100,000. However, my stock is down 75%!

How is that possible? In theory, the company makes on each BTC that it mines and owns a HODL of BTC. I thought BTC miners were a levered play on the price of BTC? What did I miss? And how can I prevent this from occurring again?

I believe the answer is that I failed to understand the basic economics of the industry: that the all in costs to mine a BTC exceed the cost of BTC. I incorrectly assumed that BTC mining would be similar to oil refining and gold mining. In oil refining, profitability is determined by the crack spread, or the difference between oil costs and revenues from refined products, like gasoline. Except for rare time periods like March 2020 when oil prices went negative, refiners are almost guaranteed to make a small profit margin from refining. So, refiners make a small profit over a continual volume of throughput. For gold miners, profitability is usually determined by the all in sustaining costs (AISC) of the mine. Gold miners cannot control the price of gold, and mining is highly capital intensive. So gold miners ensure there is a sufficient buffer between the price of gold and their AISC. Therefore, miners can cut production to maintain profitability if the price of gold declines. So, gold miners make profit based on an estimated volume of production.

BTC miners are somewhat a combination of both refiners and gold miners. Net margins are very thin (or negative) similar to refiners. But unlike gold miners, BTC miners cannot control their AISC because of the growth in global hash rate erodes the value of the mine. It’s like a gold mine, where the amount of gold in the ground has a half life and dissipates with time.

Miners also believe they grow their way to profitability by improving efficiency and economies of scale. The logic is, “gross margins are good, and we’ll eventually make money if we grow to spread the computational costs out across a wider base of operations.” Growth in global hash rates also requires them to pursue growth, so it seems to make sense. As a result, miners sell each BTC at a loss while trying to make up the loss on volume. This strategy consumes capital from investors who mistakenly believe that miners are a levered play on the price of BTC, and that miners will continue to make money when only at the periods when the economics of mining make sense.

BTC miners could perform well if this BTC bull cycle continues. As described above, it really depends on how high the price of BTC goes and whether new investors, that don’t understand the industry, bid up the stocks. But, if or when the cycle ends, miners will likely lose money as the economics of the industry don’t make sense. Time will tell.

At the very least, I got a laugh recently when I read that Hive was expanding production… by buying a site from Bitfarms (BITF), another BTC miner! Some things never change, I guess.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XMMO: Using A Momentum Strategy For Midcaps To Outperform The Market
data.description:
Introduction

Nowadays, where SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY) trades at very high multiples, many people are searching for cheaper alternatives. While some investors opt for companies outside the U.S., many seek stocks with a lower market capitalization.

In the following article, I will explain why it makes sense to invest in midcaps and why utilizing a momentum strategy via Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:XMMO) might be a good idea.

ETF Strategy and Portfolio Analysis

XMMO runs a momentum strategy on the S&P 400 Mid-Cap index. A momentum strategy means buying stocks that have performed well over the past with the hopes that they continue to do well in the future. In XMMO, the strategy is used by selecting the 80 best-performing S&P 400 Mid-Cap index stocks.

In contrast to SPY, which has more than 33% of its exposure in technology companies, XXMO has only 7% of its exposure in this sector. Nevertheless, it has big concentrations in 3 big sectors, namely industrials (27.34%), financials (27.18%), and consumer cyclical (18.57%). All other sectors have an exposure of less than 10%. Additionally, no single stock has an allocation of more than 5%, with Williams-Sonoma (WSM) being the biggest one at 4.94%. Since the ETF only holds 80 positions at a time, one can say that overweighting some stocks to that extent does not damage the diversification effect. Also, it makes sense for a momentum ETF to have the biggest positions in the stocks that have the highest momentum, as this means a higher expected return.

Costs and Peer Group Comparison

XMMO is, overall, a very cheap ETF, with an expense ratio of only 0.34% vs. an industry average of 0.49%. Further on, the ETF bid/ask spread is at only 0.06% far cheaper than the average of 0.14%. This gives the ETF the quant rating B in the expense category here on Seeking Alpha.

Also, the ETF outperforms other mid-cap momentum ETFs like Invesco S&P MidCap Value with Momentum ETF (XMVM) and growth options like Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (VOT) on a 5-year basis by a wide margin of more than 20%.

The Case for U.S. Small Caps

One of the main reasons for U.S. small caps is the valuation. While large caps currently trade on very high multiples, mid and small caps trade on much lower multiples, as can be seen in Figure 1. Additionally, mid and small caps trade at around the same multiples, but small caps showed both lower returns and a higher drawdown in the past as can be seen in Figure 2, showing the performance ratios of small caps, mid-caps (via MDY) and large caps (via SPY).

Meanwhile, the U.S. is still a great country to invest in as it shows solid growth and is the current world power.

The Reason Momentum is Especially Beneficial for Mid-caps

In their paper “What Works on European Markets”, Phillip Vanstraceele and Tim Du Toit, test different ratios on European markets between 1999 and 2011 to find out what works. One result of this is Figure 3 and Figure 4.

As can be seen, midcaps in the three highest quintiles performed best for both a 6-month and 12-month index. Also, they outperform large caps, which is especially great since we have just seen in Figure 2 that the drawdown of mid-caps has been about the same as that of large caps.

XMMO does exactly what was described in the study, picking the top 80 out of 400 companies which would equal Q1 in Figure 3 and Figure 4.

Although the study was conducted in the European markets, it should also have implications for American stocks, since both markets follow the same structure and are strongly correlated. That momentum also works on the American markets can also be proven by the ETF's history itself. In Figure 5, one can find the performance of XMMO vs. the S&P MidCap 400 Index. As can be seen, XMMO performed on every time frame using the momentum strategy.

Figure 6 also shows the ETF's long-term performance against a normal mid-cap ETF and SPY. XMMO outperformed both ETFs and showed a higher Sharpe ratio than the normal mid-cap ETF.

Risks

While momentum strategies give higher returns, they are also slightly riskier. Although XMMO did not have a bigger drawdown than SPY and a normal mid-cap ETF, it has a higher volatility that makes the strategy less suitable for conservative investors.

Additionally, there is a small concentration risk due to the three big sector exposures. While this might be a concern, the historical volatility and drawdown show that it does not have a very big effect. However, if one sector dominates the investment landscape, concentration risk could increase as the ETF would buy more of the better-performing stocks from that sector. Because of this, it makes sense to monitor the asset allocation of the ETF while holding it.

Conclusion

Using a momentum strategy in mid-caps is a great possibility for investors thinking the S&P 500 is too expensive at the moment. Using the momentum strategy, investors can outperform both normal mid- and large-cap indices to get better risk-adjusted returns than a normal mid-cap strategy would achieve.

data.title: Vestas Wind Systems: A Buying Opportunity Amid Market Pessimism
data.description:
Investment thesis

Vestas Wind Systems (OTCPK:VWSYF) (OTCPK:VWDRY) has ended 2024 with a very strong operating performance, delivering €6.1 billion in revenue and more than tripling its EBIT before special items to €759 million in Q4 2024, significantly surpassing market expectations. Despite its improved operational performance, Vestas' stock has suffered a sharp decline, losing nearly 50% of its market capitalization over the past year. Investor sentiment has turned overly pessimistic, especially following the US election. With shares now trading at the lower end of their historical valuation range and market expectations failing to price in any potential upside from continued operational strength, we believe this presents an attractive buying opportunity.

2024 results and 2025 outlook

Vestas Wind Systems A/S reported strong financial results for the full year 2024, with revenues reaching €17.3 billion, an increase from €15.38 billion in 2023. The company also significantly improved its operating profitability, with EBIT before special items rising to €741 million, compared to €231 million in the prior year. The EBIT margin for 2024 stood at 4.3%, up from 1.5% in 2023, reflecting operational efficiency improvements and higher profitability in its order backlog.

The fourth quarter of 2024 was particularly robust, with Vestas generating €6.1 billion in revenue and an EBIT margin before special items of 12.9% that helped drive the full-year improvement. Vestas has given a double-digit margin target for its business during its investor day in 2021, and it is encouraging to see that the business is returning to a level of profitability it achieved prior 2019.

With an operating profit before special items of €759 million, Vestas surpassed market expectations of €672 million. The company also reported strong free cash flow of EUR 1.1 billion for the full year, which, we assume, has given management the confidence to reinstate a dividend and introduce a share buyback program.

Despite these strong results, Vestas’ shares faced initial selling pressure on the day of the earnings announcement, dropping more than 10% intraday. The decline appeared to be driven by the company’s 2025 EBIT margin guidance of 4-7%, which fell short of market expectations. However, during the investor call, Vestas explained that the lower EBIT guidance was primarily due to the ramp-up of production for its new V236 offshore turbine. This clarification reassured investors, leading to a recovery in the stock price. By the end of the trading day, Vestas shares closed 2.8% higher.

Legacy backlog and political headwinds weighed on stock price

In recent years, VWSYF has faced significant challenges, impacting its financial performance and weighing on the stock price. A primary concern has been the execution of legacy contracts in the Power Solutions Business signed prior to the COVID-19 pandemic. These agreements proved not to be suitable for the subsequent supply chain disruptions and inflationary pressures that emerged during the pandemic era. As a result, fulfilling these contracts over the past few years has adversely affected Vestas' profitability.

In 2019, the company reported an EBIT margin of approximately 8.27%. However, as the pandemic's effects took hold, this margin began to decline, hitting its low point at about -11% in 2022.

In 2023, Vestas managed to return to profitability, although just barely, achieving an EBIT margin before special items of 1.5%. However, it seemed that the company was on its way back to restore profitability fully as it hit another speed bump in 2024. Due to cost overruns, it had to take a write-down in its services business in the second quarter of 2024. EBIT before special items stood at a negative €185 million, resulting in a margin of -5.6%. The market did not take this lightly, as it undermined trust in the management's margin targets for the year.

Following Donald Trump's election victory in November 2024, the renewable energy sector in general and Vestas in particular took another beating. Trump's known opposition to wind energy and his commitment to eliminating offshore wind projects in the U.S. have raised concerns about potential adverse effects on Vestas' operations in the American market. In response to these developments, Vestas' share price experienced a sharp decline, reflecting investor apprehension about the company's future prospects in the U.S. As of this writing, VWSYF share price has declined by more than 47% within the last 12 months.

Business performance decoupled from share price development

Strong order backlog and restored profitability in the Power Solutions Business

Despite market pessimism following recent election results, one has to acknowledge that Vestas has delivered a robust operational performance in the last quarter, defying the sharp decline in its stock valuation.

The company reported a strong order intake of €6.5 billion in Q4 2024, pushing its total backlog to a €31.6 billion. With total orders reaching 6.5 GW at an average selling price of €1.18 million per MW in Q4 2024, Vestas anticipates revenues of €7.7 billion from these contracts — a meaningful number representing 38% of its total 2024 revenues.

The company has come a long way in restoring its profitability after suffering double-digit negative EBIT margins in 2022. In Q4 2024, Vestas achieved its long-term goal of a double-digit EBIT margin, reporting a 12.9% margin for the quarter. This is noteworthy given the milestone was reached even though the company was still executing the last of its low-margin legacy projects levels and the smaller offshore business was weighing on profitability.

Average selling prices trending up

Investors have long been wary of price wars in the wind turbine industry, where manufacturers have historically competed aggressively for market share. However, concerns over a lack of pricing discipline should be easing now. While Vestas’ reported average selling prices fluctuate each quarter due to variations in order mix — particularly between onshore and offshore projects, as well as regional differences — pricing is shown an encouraging trend over the past three and a half years. This can be seen as a shift toward more sustainable pricing strategies within the industry.

Review of Service business completed in 2024

Vestas’ service segment, traditionally a stable profit driver, faced a major setback in Q2 2024 after a significant write-off led to a sharp decline in margins. The company employs percentage-of-completion (POC) accounting for its service business, recognizing revenue based on incurred costs rather than billed amounts. This approach is not without controversy as it can temporarily boost revenues when incurred costs exceed initial estimates. But if total planned costs rise—reducing profit margins—revenues need to be adjusted downward. This was the case in Q2 2024, when higher-than-expected costs triggered a negative revenue adjustment.

This was a major setback for investor sentiment and confidence into the management team, which had to be addressed. Consequently, management launched a full review of the business. By the fourth quarter of 2024, the measures taken contributed to a notable improvement in profitability, with the service business returning to an 18% EBIT margin.

During the earnings call, management expressed confidence in achieving its long term EBIT margin target in the mid-20% range once again. A target that should be achievable given that the business generated an EBIT margin as high as 27.6% in 2021.
